meaning: to ache all over/to hurt everywhere
communicative_purpose: Describing generalized body pain, often from illness or exhaustion
trigger_situations: During flu, after physical exertion, upon waking with body aches
contexts: Medical consultations, describing illness, casual complaints about health
variations: avoir mal partout, être courbatu (similar), avoir des douleurs générales
common_mistakes: Confusing word order; using 'partout' without 'mal'; forgetting articles
collocates: avoir mal partout, j'ai mal partout, mal partout dans le corps
example_2: 'avoir mal partout' est une expression utile.
example_3_translation: I know I have pain everywhere.

meaning: to be in good shape/to be fit
communicative_purpose: Describing good physical or mental condition, feeling energetic
trigger_situations: When feeling healthy, after exercise, describing one's condition
contexts: Daily conversations about health, fitness contexts, greetings
variations: être en forme, être en bonne forme, être en pleine forme (stronger)
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'en forme' meaning 'shaped' like a form; using 'avoir' instead of 'être'
collocates: être en forme, je suis en forme, très en forme
example_2: 'être en forme' est une expression utile.
example_3_translation: I know how to be in shape.

communicative_purpose: Indicating elevated body temperature, usually due to illness
trigger_situations: During infection, when feeling unwell with fever symptoms, in medical context
contexts: Doctor's visits, describing illness to employer, caring for sick person
variations: avoir la fièvre, avoir de la fièvre, avoir un peu de fièvre
common_mistakes: Confusing article usage; confusing with avoir chaud (feeling hot)
collocates: avoir la fièvre, il a la fièvre, faire monter la fièvre
example_2: 'avoir la fièvre' est une expression utile.
example_2_translation: 'have a fever' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know I have a fever.

communicative_purpose: Describing injury to the ankle joint from twisting
trigger_situations: After falling, during sports, after stepping awkwardly
contexts: Sports injuries, describing accidents, medical consultations
variations: se fouler la cheville, se tordre la cheville (similar), se casser la cheville (more serious)
common_mistakes: Confusing with other body parts; confusing with 'se fouler le poignet'
collocates: se fouler la cheville, il s'est foulé la cheville, foulure à la cheville
example_2: 'se fouler la cheville' est une expression utile.
example_3: Je connais se fouler la cheville.
example_2_translation: 'sprain your ankle' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know how to sprain an ankle.

communicative_purpose: Indicating pain in the head area, from tension, illness, or fatigue
trigger_situations: During stress, during illness, after late nights, with allergies
contexts: Daily health complaints, medical contexts, explaining not feeling well
variations: avoir mal à la tête, avoir la migraine (more serious), avoir mal à la tête
common_mistakes: Forgetting 'à la'; confusing with 'tête' meaning head as concept; using wrong preposition
collocates: avoir mal à la tête, j'ai mal à la tête, une forte tête
example_2: 'avoir mal à la tête' est une expression utile.
example_3: Je connais avoir mal à la tête.
example_2_translation: 'have a headache' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know I have a headache.

communicative_purpose: Describing extreme fatigue, often from overwork or illness
trigger_situations: After long work, after illness, during difficult periods
contexts: Workplace conversations, describing health, casual settings
variations: être épuisé, être épuisée (feminine), être exténué (stronger)
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'épuiser' (to exhaust) as verb; confusing exhaustion levels
collocates: être épuisé, je suis épuisé, complètement épuisé

communicative_purpose: Describing nasal discharge, usually from cold or allergies
trigger_situations: During colds, during allergy season, when ill
contexts: Describing cold symptoms, medical contexts, casual health conversations
variations: avoir le nez qui coule, avoir le rhume (broader), éternuer (action)
common_mistakes: Confusing with other nose expressions; using wrong verb tense
collocates: avoir le nez qui coule, mon nez coule, le nez qui coule
example_2: 'avoir le nez qui coule' est une expression utile.
example_3: Je connais avoir le nez qui coule.
example_2_translation: 'having a runny nose' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know I have a runny nose.

communicative_purpose: Describing increase in body weight, often from lifestyle changes
trigger_situations: After holidays, during sedentary periods, when discussing health
contexts: Health discussions, fitness contexts, medical consultations
variations: prendre du poids, grossir (verb), prendre du poids rapidement
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'perdre du poids' (lose weight); using wrong article
collocates: prendre du poids, j'ai pris du poids, trop de poids
example_2: 'prendre du poids' est une expression utile.
example_3_translation: I know how to gain weight.

communicative_purpose: Describing body weight that exceeds healthy range
trigger_situations: During medical checkups, when discussing health and diet
contexts: Medical contexts, fitness discussions, health consultations
variations: être en surpoids, être obèse (more serious), avoir des kilos en trop
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'poids' alone; not knowing the medical term
collocates: être en surpoids, être légèrement en surpoids, calculer le surpoids
example_2: 'être en surpoids' est une expression utile.

communicative_purpose: Describing sensation of dizziness or room spinning
trigger_situations: After standing quickly, during illness, from fear of heights
contexts: Medical consultations, describing symptoms, emergency situations
variations: avoir des vertiges, avoir le vertige (singular form), être sujet aux vertiges
common_mistakes: Confusing singular/plural; confusing with English 'vertigo' meaning fear of heights
collocates: avoir des vertiges, les vertiges, causes des vertiges
example_2: 'avoir des vertiges' est une expression utile.

communicative_purpose: Describing self-inflicted injury or general injury
trigger_situations: During sports, at work, after accidents
contexts: Sports contexts, workplace safety, accident reports
variations: se blesser, se faire mal, se blesser au travail
common_mistakes: Confusing reflexive form; using 'blesser' without reflexive for self-injury
collocates: se blesser, il s'est blessé, leicht se blesser
example_3_translation: I know how to get hurt.

meaning: to have insomnia/to suffer from sleeplessness
communicative_purpose: Describing difficulty sleeping, inability to fall or stay asleep
trigger_situations: During stressful periods, with anxiety, due to health issues
contexts: Medical consultations, describing health problems, casual conversations
variations: avoir des insomnies, avoir des problèmes de sommeil, ne pas dormir
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'insomnie' singular; using wrong article
collocates: avoir des insomnies, souffrir d'insomnies, insomnies chroniques
example_2: 'avoir des insomnies' est une expression utile.

communicative_purpose: Describing overall healthy condition, absence of illness
trigger_situations: During checkups, in response to health inquiries, in medical contexts
contexts: Medical consultations, daily greetings, insurance contexts
variations: être en bonne santé, être en bonne forme (similar), être en santé
common_mistakes: Forgetting article 'bonne'; confusing 'santé' with other languages' forms
collocates: être en bonne santé, en parfaite santé, bonne santé générale
example_2: 'être enbonne santé' est une expression utile.
example_3_translation: I know how to be healthy.

meaning: to be emotionally sensitive/to have a fragile heart
communicative_purpose: Describing emotional vulnerability or sensitivity to rejection
trigger_situations: After emotional hurt, during difficult times, when describing someone's nature
contexts: Personal discussions, describing character traits, relationship contexts
variations: avoir le cœur fragile, avoir le cœur sensible (similar), être sensible
common_mistakes: Confusing with literal heart conditions; using wrong article placement
collocates: avoir le cœur fragile, elle a le cœur fragile, un cœur fragile
example_2: 'avoir le cœur fragile' est une expression utile.
example_3: Je connais avoir le cœur fragile.
example_2_translation: 'to be faint of heart' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know I have a fragile heart.

communicative_purpose: Describing taking time to recover, often due to illness or fatigue
trigger_situations: After illness, during recovery, when tired
contexts: Health contexts, workplace (sick leave), daily conversations
variations: se reposer, dormir, prendre du repos, être au repos
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'dormir' (sleep) which is different; using wrong reflexive form
collocates: se reposer, je me repose, reposez-vous
